coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and selective aptasensor for detection of adenosine based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer from carbon dots to nano-graphite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xu
Xu, Guanhong
Wei, Fangdi
Ma, Yunsu
Ma, Yujie
Song, Yueyue
Cen, Yao
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: In this article, a novel aptasensor was fabricated by modifying carbon dots (CDs) with adenosine aptamer (CDs-aptamer) for sensitive, selective and quantitative detection of adenosine (AD). When nano-graphite (NG) as an energy acceptor was added into the CDs-aptamer (energy donor) solution, the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer was quenched due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). When AD was present in the solution of CDs-aptamer/NG, the process of FRET was inhibited because of the specific combination between AD and AD aptamer. As a result, the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer was restored due to the dissociation of CDs-aptamer from NG and its change was proportional to the AD concentration. Under the optimized conditions, a linear range was found to be 2–50nM for the detection of AD with a detection limit of 0.63nM. Furthermore, the application of the proposed approach was demonstrated in real sample with satisfying results and it showed promise in diagnostic purpose.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aptamer
Adenosine
Nano-graphite
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

Specificity of the aptasensor for AD
Determination of AD in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectrum of NG (a) and fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs-aptamer (b).
Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-aptamer (a), CDs-aptamer/NG (b) and CDs-aptamer/NG added with AD (c).

Characterizations of CDs: (A) TEM images; (B) FTIR spectrum; (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum (a), fluorescence excitation (b), and emission (c) spectrum. (D) Time scan fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs excited at 366nm.
Agarose gel electrophoresis of aptamer modified CDs. Lane 1: CDs-aptamer, Lane2: Aptamer.
The selectivity and anti-interference experiments; including analogues and coexistent ions, biological molecules in urine.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-aptamer/NG aptasensor in the presence of different concentrations of AD (from a to g: 0, 2.00, 5.00, 10.00, 25.00, 50.00, 60.00nM). Inset: calibration curve: fluorescence intensity changes [(F−F0)/F0] versus AD (2–50nM).
Schematic illustration of turn-on AD sensor based on CDs-aptamer and NG.
The intra-day and inter-day precision of the determination of AD (n=3).
Determination of AD in urine sample (n=3).
Comparison of the method built with other methods for the determination of AD.

row.entry: Sensor derived from a triplex DNA aptamer
Aptasensor based on FRET fromCdS QDs to polypyrrole
Aptasensor based on FRET fromcarbon dots to nano-graphite

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and selective aptasensor for detection of adenosine based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer from carbon dots to nano-graphite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, a novel aptasensor was fabricated by modifying carbon dots (CDs) with adenosine aptamer (CDs-aptamer) for sensitive, selective and quantitative detection of adenosine (AD). When nano-graphite (NG) as an energy acceptor was added into the CDs-aptamer (energy donor) solution, the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer was quenched due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). When AD was present in the solution of CDs-aptamer/NG, the process of FRET was inhibited because of the specific combination between AD and AD aptamer. As a result, the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer was restored due to the dissociation of CDs-aptamer from NG and its change was proportional to the AD concentration. Under the optimized conditions, a linear range was found to be 2–50nM for the detection of AD with a detection limit of 0.63nM. Furthermore, the application of the proposed approach was demonstrated in real sample with satisfying results and it showed promise in diagnostic purpose.

ce:section.ce:para: The selective, sensitive and cost-effective detection of disease-related biomolecules in biological fluids has attracted much attention because of their potential applications in clinical diagnostics and treatment. Human adenosine (AD) plays an important role in the human body physiological activities [1–3]. For example, AD can regulate heart myocardial oxygen consumption and blood flow, and modulate the release of brain neurotransmitters such as dopamine via AD receptors [4]. In addition, it can help to regulate smooth muscle contraction, neurotransmission, renal hemo-dynamics and the release of rennin, etc. [5]. Under normal physiological conditions, traces of AD are released into extracellular spaces to carry out its important pathophysiological role. However, a great quantity of AD is released into body fluid/blood when the oxygen-supply balance is disturbed [6]. So it is very necessary to detect AD for clinical diagnosis.

In recent years, different methods have been developed to determine AD, such as high performance liquid chromatography [7], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [8], electrochemiluminescence [9] and fluorescence quantum dots (QDs) aptasensor [10,11]. Among these methods, QDs-based aptasensors have attracted lots of interests because of the excellent fluorescence properties of QDs and the specificity of aptamer. For example, the use of aptamer allows QDs aptasensors to be highly selective because aptamers are artificial single-chain DNA or RNA oligonucleotides which show high affinity to specific targets, generated by an in vitro process, termed as the systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment [12,13]. QDs [14,15] are also extensively utilized due to their low photobleaching, good chemical stability and tunable spectral properties compared with fluorescence organic dyes [16]. However, QDs have some drawbacks as fluorescence probe, such as long preparation time, cumbersome preparation process, heavy metal pollution and so on [17]. Therefore, it is indispensable to adopt a new type of fluorescent material to solve the problems of fluorescence probe based on QDs.

Nowadays, carbon dots (CDs) have gained tremendous attention due to captivating properties such as good water solubility, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent photostability and strong fluorescence [18–20]. As a new kind of carbon nano-particles, CDs were first discovered in the purification procedures of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [21]. Typically, CDs with size below 10nm display size-dependent fluorescence properties. The fluorescence from CDs may be attributed to the presence of surface energy traps that become emissive upon stabilization as a result of surface passivation. According to reports, the quantum yields of CDs can be greatly improved by surface passivation [22]. And owing to a large amount of amino and carboxyl on the surface of CDs, CDs can be further modified for broad applications such as bioimaging [23] and sensing [24]. However, most sensors using functionalized CDs as probes are based on turn-off principle, which lack high sensitivity due to strong matrix effects. So it is urgently required to develop CDs sensors based on turn-on principle. In recent years, nano-graphite (NG) has been reported as a good quencher to build turn-on sensors [25,26].

NG has been used for ideal energy acceptor because of similar sp2 electronic structure to that of graphene, and broad absorption bands [25]. In addition, NG shows good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. All of these provide great application prospects in wide fields. Moreover, the detection of AD based on aptamer modified CDs and NG has not been reported.

In this work, we set up a novel method to detect AD sensitively, selectively, rapidly and simply. First, CDs with high quantum yield were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Then, selecting AD as the analyte, an aptasensor was constructed by modifying CDs with AD aptamer to form CDs-aptamer complex. NG which could turn off the fluorescence of the aptasensor was mixed with the CDs-aptamer complex. When AD was introduced into the CDs-aptamer/NG system, AD specifically combined with the AD aptamer linked with CDs and the fluorescence of the aptasensor was turned on. Based on the restored fluorescence, a homogeneous turn-on assay for AD was proposed.

Citric acid monohydrate, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), ascorbicacid (AA), Urea (U) and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Diethylene triamine was purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). AD, guanosine (GS), cytidine (CD) and uridine (UD) with purity greater than 99% and the 5′-amino-modified DNA oligonucleotides (5′-ACCTGGGGGAGTATTGCGGAGGAAGGT-3′) for the antiadenosine aptamer with a C6 spacer arm were purchased in Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The NG (<30nm) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10mM, pH 7.4). The purified water used in the study was prepared by Direct-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA). All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

Fourier-transform infrared spectra were recorded on a TENSOR 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) using pelletizing a homogenized powder of the synthesized nanoparticles and KBr with a resolution of 2cm−1 and a spectral range of 4000–400cm−1. The fluorescence spectra were collected with an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopic and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were acquired on a JEOL JEM−2100 (HR) transmission electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan). The combination between aptamer and CDs was confirmed by 6.0% agarose gel electrophoresis (Shanghai Tanon HE-120) with 0.5×TBE buffer at 100V for 60min.

CDs were synthesized according to a reported method with a little modification [27]. Briefly, citric acid monohydrate (1.2g) and diethylene triamine (1200μL) were added into 20mL of ultrapure water. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave (30mL), which was placed in a drying oven followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200°C for 4h. When cooled down to room temperature, the pH of the product was adjusted to 7 and was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for 18h. The final solution in the dialysis bag was collected and dried by a vacuum oven. The solid was redissolved in PBS with a concentration of 20μg/mL. The CDs solution was stored as stock solution.

The conjugations between CDs and aptamer were prepared by well-established carbodiimide chemistry [28]. 30μL of 1mg/μL EDC in 10mM PBS was added to 100μL of CDs solution and the mixture was sonicated for 15min. Afterwards, 50μL of the mixture was added to 450μL of 2μM AD aptamer in 10mM PBS, and further incubated for 2h at 25°C.

The determination of AD was operated as follows. 50μL of NG (0.45mg/mL) and 50μL PBS was added into 50μL of aforementioned CDs-aptamer solution, and the mixture was incubated for 10min at 25°C. Afterwards, 50μL of AD standard solution was introduced into the reaction system and the mixture was shaken by the shaker for 1.5h at 25°C. The signal output values were calculated according to the relative fluorescence intensity [(F−F0)/F0], in which F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-aptamer/NG solution excited at 366nm in the presence and absence of AD, respectively.

To further prove the reliability and practicality of the proposed method for AD detection, the standard addition method was used for detection of AD in urine samples which were obtained from laboratory healthy volunteer. Urine samples were first filtered by a 0.22μm filter membrane, then filtrate was diluted 80-fold using PBS solution. Finally, the diluted urine samples spiked with different concentrations of AD were detected as described in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the as-prepared biosensor for AD detection
ce:section.ce:para: Schematic illustration of AD detection strategy based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and NG is displayed in Scheme 1 . First of all, the surface of CDs was modified with AD aptamer to form CDs-aptamer aptasensor according to the well-established carbodiimide chemistry. In the absence of AD, CDs-aptamer would be adsorbed on the surface of NG via π–π interaction between the aptamer and NG due to the sp2 electronic hybrid structure of NG. As a result, the energy donor CDs-aptamer and the energy acceptor NG were taken into close proximity. As is well known that FRET would occur when there is an overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor and the UV absorption spectrum of the acceptor and the distance between donor and acceptor is less than 7nm [29]. As shown in Fig. 1 , NG had broad absorption from 200 to 800nm and its absorption spectrum overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs-aptamer. Thus, when CDs-aptamer was absorbed on the surface of NG, the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer would be quenched (turn-off state) due to FRET. However,with the introduction of AD to the as-formed CDs-aptamer/NG system, AD would specifically combine with the aptamer capped on the surface of CDs to form a double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) with a recessed 3′-terminus due to its structural switching property [6], which would lead to the disappearance of the π–π stacking interaction. Eventually, CDs-aptamer was released from NG. As a result, the FRET process was inhibited and the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer was recovered (turn-on state). As shown in Fig. 2 , the experiment principle is well confirmed by the experimental results.

The morphology and structure of CDs were verified by several analyses. TEM characterization of Fig. 3 A showed that CDs were spherical, well-dispersed and approximately 3–6nm in size with the most probable diameter of 4.6nm. The inset in Fig. 3A showed the lattice spacing distance of 0.227nm, which agreed well with the 002 spacing of graphitic carbon [30], and was similar to other previously reported C-dots [31].

We then applied Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to further characterize the properties of chemical bonds and functional group on the surface of CDs. In Fig.3B, the broad vibration band at 3400cm−1 corresponded to OH and NH stretching vibrations. CDs also had the characteristic peak around at 2988cm−1 due to the OH of carboxyl bonding vibration. In addition, the peaks around 2895 and 1440cm−1 were accordance with the vibrations of CH3. Two characteristic peaks centered at 1660cm−1 and 1550cm−1 were assigned to the vibrations of CO and NH, respectively. According to FTIR spectra, it could be deduced that the surfaces of CDs should be occupied with hydrophilic groups (OH and COOH) and N-related groups (NH2 and CONH).

In Fig. 3C, CDs showed two absorption bands at 242 and 355nm, which were originated from the π–π∗ transition of the aromatic ring structure and the n–π∗ transition of the CO group [32], respectively. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the CDs were 366 and 452nm as shown in Fig. 3C. When excited at 366nm, the CDs showed very strong fluorescence in the range of 380–600nm, with the maximum emission wavelength located at around 452nm.

To test the optical stability of CDs, CDs were excited at 366nm, 550V for 150min. The fluorescence intensity of CDs had no remarkable change which is shown in Fig. 3D. It showed that CDs owned good stability against photobleaching.

The production of aptamer modified CDs was confirmed by gel retardation assay [33]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the band of CDs-aptamer was retarded compared to aptamer band. It could be concluded that the surface of CDs had been modified with AD aptamer successfully.

In order to get the best AD sensing performance, the experimental conditions including media pH, incubation temperature, aptamer concentration, reaction time of conjunction between aptamer and CDs, NG concentration, quenching time between CDs-aptamer and NG, incubation time and temperature of the combination between CDs-aptamer/NG and AD were optimized.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence of CDs in the range of 1–12 was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence of CDs gradually enhanced when the pH was increased from 1 to 7. When pH >7, CDs had maximum fluorescence and remained stable. It could be explained that when CDs were under acidic conditions, CDs would quickly self-assemble into larger particles and surface oxygen-related groups of CDs could be slowly oxidized at room temperature so that the fluorescence intensity quenched [34]. Thus, PBS buffer with a pH value of 7.4 was selected for experimentation.
The effect of temperature on the conjunction between CDs and aptamer was evaluated in 4, 25 and 37°C. Experimental results (Fig. S1B) showed that when in 25°C, CDs-aptamer had maximum fluorescence.

To assess the effect of AD aptamer concentration on the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer, increasing concentrations of AD aptamer (0–2.0μM) were added into activated CDs solution, respectively. The results (Fig. S1C) showed that the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer gradually enhanced when the concentration of AD aptamer was increased from 0 to 0.5μM. However, the fluorescence of CDs-aptamer decreased gradually when the concentration of AD aptamer was increased from 0.5 to 2.0μM. It may be that excessive aptamer which has long chain structure blocked out the fluorescence site.

To investigate the effect of incubation time on the combination of CDs-aptamer complex, the combinations between CDs and aptamer were incubated for different time from 0 to 4h. The results (Fig. S1D) showed that 2h was the best incubation time.

To determine the optimum concentration of NG for the efficient quenching with CDs-aptamer, increasing concentrations of NG (0, 0.15, 0.25, 0.35, 0.45, 0.55, 0.65, 0.75mg/mL) were added to a fix concentration of CDs-aptamer. Experimental results (Fig. S2A) showed that when the concentration of NG rose to 0.45mg/mL, it could induce the relatively maximum fluorescence decrease. Though increasing concentration of NG would cause further decrease, however, NG would aggregate to lager particles easily. So, 0.45mg/mL was chosen as the best addition concentration of NG.

The time-dependent response was carried out to investigate a suitable incubation time for NG quenching the probe fluorescence, which is shown in Fig. S2B. When 0.45mg/mL of NG were added into CDs-aptamer solution and incubated for 10min, the fluorescence of the probe was quenched furthest, and a plateau was observed after prolonging time. Therefore, 10min was adopted as the optimal incubation time.

The effects of incubation time (0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0h) and temperature (4, 25, 37°C) on fluorescence recovery when AD was added to the mixture of CDs-aptamer/NG were also assessed. As shown in Fig. S2C and Fig. S2D, 1.5h and 25°C were chosen as the optimal incubation time and temperature, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the aptasensor for AD
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the developed aptasensor was also tested. Three other analogues including GS, UD and CD and other common coexistent ions in urine sample, biological molecules were utilized to verify the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the strategy. Fig. 5 showed the interference of analogues, various ions and molecules, including GS, UD, CD with a concentration of 50nM, AA, UA, U, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na2+ with a concentration of 1μM, Glu with a concentration of 4mM and Pb2+ with a concentration of 100nM. According to Fig. 5, only AD with a concentration of 50nM could cause remarkable change in fluorescence intensity. Other substances could only cause changes less than 5%. These results indicated that the developed method possessed sufficient selectivity for AD detection.

Under the optimal condition, a linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence change ((F–F0)/F0) of CDs-aptamer/NG and the AD amount over a range of 2–50nM as shown in Fig. 6 . The regression equation could be expressed as (F–F0)/F0 =0.10477Ln [AD] + 0.00646 (AD represented the concentration of adenosine, nM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9925, and the limit of detection (LOD) for AD was 0.63nM based on 3σ/κ (σ is the standard deviation of ten blank measurement of CDs-aptamer/NG, and k is the slope of the regression line). As shown in Table 1 , the intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviations (RSDs) of three different concentrations were all within 5.9%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AD in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The developed aptasensor was utilized to measure AD in human urine samples. The results are listed in Table 2 . The recoveries for AD in human urine samples were in the range of 94.5–107.3% with RSDs less than 8.3%, indicating that the designed aptasensor could be used to precisely determine AD concentration in biological sample.

A sensitive and selective method for quantitative detection of AD using aptamer-conjugated CDs as a novel aptasensor based on FRET from CDs to NG was successfully established in this paper. The LOD for AD was calculated to be as low as 0.63nM. Compared with other methods for detection of AD which are listed in Table 3 , the LOD of this method established in this paper is lowest. Moreover, this proposed aptasensor has two main advantages: (1) The preparation and application of aptasensor are convenient, rapid and simple without complex operations. (2) All materials used in this work are low toxicity or non-toxic, which can avoid environmental pollution. The designed assay was applied to the determination of AD in human urine sample with satisfactory accuracy and precision, indicating its prospect in clinical diagnosis. Using other aptamers instead of AD aptamer, this strategy may offer a suitable approach for detection of other biomarkers in biological samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 81173016].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.07.028.
